narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your family's low income bracket, and we appreciate the strength that brings to your journey. The admissions model evaluated your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 35, Year 1 school decile of 3, Year 3 decile of 2, full-time student status, and background— to predict your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. It estimated a 63% probability that you would fail, which unfortunately crossed the threshold for rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed right away. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.53 average, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also lower than the average of 5.71, added to this concern as the next strongest influence in the same direction. On a more positive note, your LSAT score, though slightly under the 36.70 average, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, much like your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, which was close to the typical 3.21 and helped balance things out somewhat. Your low family income had a minor push toward failure, while planning to attend full-time— the norm for 93% of students— slightly supported a passing outcome. Overall, your profile stands out from typical applicants with stronger decile rankings, placing you at higher risk in the model's eyes despite some solid metrics. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law, but rather how these academic indicators stacked up. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or programs, and highlighting any resilience from your background in a revised application or appeal. Many students in similar situations improve these areas and succeed— you're capable of that too. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
